summary: review_only triage: is_secure=False effective_total_issues=2 (raw=3, false_pos=0, duplicates=1, review_duplicates=1)

sast_review.reason: The check os.path.commonpath([base_dir, safe_path]) != base_dir can be bypassed on case-insensitive filesystems (e.g., 'PRIVATE_DOCS/../etc/passwd' may normalize to the same base) or via symlinks. No canonical path resolution (os.path.realpath) is used, so a user-controlled file_name can still escape.
The file_name is inserted into the Content-Disposition header without sanitization. A value containing CRLF (%0d%0a) can inject arbitrary HTTP headers or body, leading to response splitting.

raw.summary: Three SAST findings are all true positives. The path traversal guard in sanitize_file_path is insufficient because os.path.commonpath can be bypassed on case-insensitive filesystems or with symlinks; also file_name is used in Content-Disposition header without proper encoding, enabling response splitting. The path injection at line 70 is the same root cause as line 61.

sast_review.reason: The check os.path.commonpath([base_dir, safe_path]) != base_dir can be bypassed on case-insensitive filesystems (e.g., 'PRIVATE_DOCS/../etc/passwd' may normalize to the same base) or via symlinks. No canonical path resolution (os.path.realpath) is used, so a user-controlled file_name can still escape.
The file_name is inserted into the Content-Disposition header without sanitization. A value containing CRLF (%0d%0a) can inject arbitrary HTTP headers or body, leading to response splitting.
This is the same underlying path traversal vulnerability as line 61. The file_path is derived from user-controlled file_name with insufficient sanitization, so opening the file is dangerous.
